name: Meanwing Finch
ml_flavor: Each bird steers toward the average position of its neighbors, showing how a simple mean can organize a whole flock.
description: A sparrow-sized bird, 14 cm from beak to tail and barely 25 g, with straw-brown plumage, blue-black wing bars, gripping feet, and a conical seed-cracking bill. Nine-bird flocks glean fallen iris seed and meadow insects by day. At alarm, each finch matches its neighbors' spacing and heading, pulling the flock toward a moving center before the whole cloud sweeps back. Wingbeats hiss like dry grass.

name: Trail-Loop Leveret
ml_flavor: It memorizes one safe route and repeats it when conditions change, showing overfitting: perfect recall without adaptation.
description: A hare-sized runner, 46 cm nose to tail and about 2 kg, with long heat-shedding ears, tawny camouflage, and broad hind feet that barely bruise wet turf. Leverets crop clover at dawn and dusk, resting in shallow grass forms by day. Families flee along one scent-marked loop they know perfectly—even when weather, new growth, or Kern has changed the route. High-set eyes give near-panoramic vision.

name: Patchmunch Bramblehog
ml_flavor: It adjusts its path after each small patch it grazes, like mini-batch learning that updates from a few examples at a time.
description: Badger-sized at 70 cm long and 18 kg, this low grazer wears coarse chestnut guard hairs that catch burrs while a dense undercoat sheds meadow rain. Its flexible snout and cloven hooves expose roots, beetle grubs, and shoots. It feeds one hoof-wide patch at a time, samples nearby scent and forage, then shuffles on; cornered, it gives a breathy snort before one short lunge. Mud beads on its belly.

name: Middleset Mossram
ml_flavor: It keeps moving toward the center of nearby grazers, acting like a cluster centroid that represents the group around it.
description: Sheep-sized—1.1 m long, 65 cm at the shoulder, and about 45 kg—the ram has a barrel chest, cloven hooves, blunt curling horns, and damp outer wool tufted with living moss. It crops grass and iris stems in daylight. By watching the bodies around it, it continually walks to the herd's geometric middle; when threatened, it plants its feet, exhales, and charges. Amber eyes have horizontal pupils.

name: Rustlewatch Prowler
ml_flavor: It predicts visitors from one feature—rustling grass—and often pounces at wind, showing the weakness of a single-feature classifier.
description: Lynx-sized, 1.2 m nose to tail and roughly 17 kg, the prowler has a striped coat, a ringed tail, silent padded paws, and tall moon-pale ears that swivel independently above the grass. It dens under boundary stones by day and stalks leverets after dark. Crouched downwind, it fixes on one rustle, then springs in an arc—accurate in calm air, comically fooled by gusts. Claws sheath during the stalk.

name: Lilywise Newt
ml_flavor: It copies the majority color of its three nearest lily pads before firing, showing how nearest neighbors classify by local company.
description: At 55 cm nose to tail and about 1.5 kg, this giant millpond newt has cool pebbled skin, four webbed feet, a finned tail, and three pairs of feathery gills rich in color-shifting chromatophores. It shelters under roots and hunts gnats and snails in rain. Before firing a harmlessly crackling data bead, its gills copy the majority hue of the three nearest lily pads, then it swims to new cover.

name: Lastlook Jackdaw
ml_flavor: It copies only the most recent action it observed, a simple form of recency bias that forgets older examples too quickly.
description: Jackdaw-sized—36 cm long, 70 cm across the wings, and about 230 g—this blue-black ruin bird has layered flight feathers, a silver nape, and pale watchful eyes. It nests in mortar gaps and feeds on grain, beetles, and pilfered honeycomb. During a standoff it copies only Kern's latest move: side-hopping after a dodge, flaring after a swing, then snapping a data bolt from its bill. Its bill clicks.

name: Brackenhoof, Keeper of Forks
ml_flavor: At each pause it asks one simple question—near or far, moving or still—then follows that branch like a decision tree.
description: Elk-sized—2.3 m long, 1.5 m at the shoulder, and about 310 kg—Brackenhoof has splayed hooves, a deep chest, forked antlers, and overlapping fern-veined shoulder scutes. Villagers' tiny stone bells hang from flax cords in his tines. He browses the ruins alone, tests distance and motion with solemn head tilts, then commits to one thunderous, clearly signaled charge. Dark dew beads on his coat.
